title: Thyroid Storm
objectives: Recognize clinical features that distinguish thyroid storm from uncomplicated thyrotoxicosis
Apply the Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale for diagnosis
Understand the multimodal treatment approach with proper sequencing
Explain the mechanisms of each therapeutic agent
Manage thyroid storm in special situations (pregnancy, beta-blocker contraindications)
Identify precipitating factors and prevent recurrence

guidelineReferences.name: StatPearls: Thyroid Storm
guidelineReferences.citation: Pokhrel B, Bhusal K. Thyroid Storm. StatPearls [Internet]. 2022 Oct 6
keyPoints: Treatment: beta-blocker, thionamide, iodine (1 hour after), steroids
PTU preferred initially due to peripheral T4→T3 conversion blockade

guidelineReferences.name: ATA 2016 Hyperthyroidism Guidelines
guidelineReferences.citation: Ross DS, et al. 2016 American Thyroid Association Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism. Thyroid. 2016;26(10):1343-1421

guidelineReferences.name: EMCrit: Thyroid Storm
guidelineReferences.citation: Farkas J. Thyroid Storm. Internet Book of Critical Care. 2024

vignette.text:
A 32-year-old woman is brought to the ED by her family for confusion and agitation. She has a history of Graves' disease diagnosed 2 years ago but stopped taking her methimazole 3 weeks ago due to pregnancy concerns (she is not pregnant). She developed upper respiratory symptoms 5 days ago, followed by fever, palpitations, and progressive confusion over the past 24 hours.

Past Medical History: Graves' disease (diagnosed 2 years ago, on methimazole until 3 weeks ago), Anxiety

Medications: None currently (stopped methimazole)

Allergies: None

Social History: Non-smoker, no alcohol

physicalExam.general: Agitated, diaphoretic, confused (oriented to person only)
physicalExam.HEENT: Lid lag, proptosis, diffuse thyroid enlargement with bruit
physicalExam.cardiovascular: Tachycardic, irregularly irregular, hyperdynamic precordium, no murmurs

diagnostics.ecg: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, rate 156

imaging.chestXray: Mild pulmonary vascular congestion, no infiltrates

questions.stem: This patient has fever 39.8°C, HR 156 with AFib, confusion, and severely elevated thyroid hormones.
questions.question: How is thyroid storm diagnosed and what distinguishes it from uncomplicated thyrotoxicosis?
questions.hint: Consider the clinical diagnosis and scoring system.

options.text: Diagnosed solely by T4 level >5x normal
Clinical diagnosis based on systemic decompensation; Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale ≥45 indicates thyroid storm; distinguished by end-organ dysfunction, not just hormone levels
Diagnosed by thyroid ultrasound findings
Requires positive thyroid antibodies

questions.explanation: Thyroid storm is a CLINICAL diagnosis. The Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale (BWPS) uses: Temperature, CNS effects, GI/hepatic dysfunction, Heart rate, Presence of AFib, Heart failure, and Precipitating event. Score ≥45 = highly suggestive of thyroid storm; 25-44 = impending storm. Key: Thyroid hormone levels don't distinguish storm from uncomplicated thyrotoxicosis - it's the systemic decompensation.

whyChain.why: What's this patient's BWPS score?
whyChain.answer: Temp 39.8°C = 25 points; HR 156 = 25 points; AFib present = 10 points; CNS (agitation/confusion) = 20 points; Precipitant (infection + medication discontinuation) = 10 points. Total = 90 points (max 140). Score ≥45 confirms thyroid storm.

whyChain.why: Why can't we rely on thyroid hormone levels alone?
whyChain.answer: Patients with severe thyrotoxicosis may have similar hormone levels without storm. Storm represents a state of end-organ decompensation where tissues can no longer compensate for excess thyroid hormone. The rate of rise and individual susceptibility matter more than absolute levels.

questions.stem: The patient stopped her methimazole 3 weeks ago and developed an upper respiratory infection.
questions.question: What are common precipitating factors for thyroid storm?
questions.hint: Consider what triggered decompensation in this patient.

options.text: Starting antithyroid medications
Infection, antithyroid drug discontinuation, surgery, iodine load (contrast), trauma, DKA, pregnancy/delivery
Hypothermia
Only surgical procedures

questions.explanation: Common precipitants: (1) Infection (most common, including COVID-19), (2) Antithyroid drug non-compliance/discontinuation, (3) Thyroid/non-thyroid surgery, (4) Iodine load (contrast, amiodarone), (5) Trauma, (6) DKA and other acute illnesses, (7) Pregnancy/delivery, (8) Radioactive iodine treatment. This patient has TWO precipitants: infection AND medication discontinuation.

whyChain.why: Why does infection trigger thyroid storm?
whyChain.answer: Infection increases metabolic demand and causes cytokine release. In patients with baseline thyrotoxicosis, this additional stress overwhelms compensatory mechanisms. Catecholamine release during illness also amplifies thyroid hormone effects.

whyChain.why: How does medication discontinuation lead to storm?
whyChain.answer: Without antithyroid drugs, thyroid hormone synthesis resumes and hormone levels rise. In Graves' disease, autoantibody-driven stimulation continues unchecked. This patient had 3 weeks of uncontrolled hormone production before the infection pushed her into storm.

questions.stem: The patient has tachycardia, wide pulse pressure, hyperthermia, and agitation.
questions.question: What is the pathophysiology of these cardiovascular and CNS manifestations?
questions.hint: Consider thyroid hormone effects on catecholamine sensitivity.

options.text: Thyroid hormone directly causes vasoconstriction
Direct toxic effect of T4 on the heart
Pheochromocytoma coexistence
Thyroid hormones increase β-adrenergic receptor expression and sensitivity, amplifying catecholamine effects; also increase metabolic rate causing hyperthermia

questions.explanation: Thyroid hormone effects: (1) Increases β-adrenergic receptor density and sensitivity → amplified response to normal catecholamines → tachycardia, tremor, anxiety; (2) Increases basal metabolic rate → hyperthermia, weight loss; (3) Increases cardiac output (↑HR, ↑contractility, ↓SVR) → wide pulse pressure; (4) CNS: direct neural effects + hyperadrenergic state → agitation, confusion, psychosis.

whyChain.why: Why do beta-blockers help if catecholamines aren't elevated?
whyChain.answer: In thyrotoxicosis, catecholamine levels are normal or even low. The problem is tissue hypersensitivity to catecholamines due to increased β-receptor expression. Beta-blockers block this amplified signal, reducing tachycardia, tremor, and anxiety. They don't lower thyroid hormone levels.

whyChain.why: Why does AFib occur?
whyChain.answer: Excess thyroid hormone shortens atrial refractory period and increases automaticity. Combined with increased sympathetic tone, this creates substrate for AFib. New-onset AFib is present in 10-25% of thyrotoxicosis patients. Her irregular rhythm (156 bpm) is AFib with RVR.

questions.stem: You are initiating treatment for this patient with thyroid storm.
questions.question: What is the correct sequence of therapies for thyroid storm?
questions.hint: Consider which drug must come before iodine and why.

options.text: Start with iodine, then add other medications
Beta-blocker alone is sufficient
[1] Beta-blocker → [2] Thionamide (PTU or methimazole) → [3] Iodine (≥1 hour after thionamide) → [4] Glucocorticoid
Glucocorticoid first, then others if needed

questions.explanation: Treatment sequence is critical: (1) Beta-blocker: immediate symptom control; (2) Thionamide: blocks NEW hormone synthesis; (3) Iodine: blocks hormone RELEASE (Wolff-Chaikoff effect) - MUST be given ≥1 hour AFTER thionamide to prevent increased hormone synthesis from iodine load; (4) Glucocorticoid: blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion, treats relative adrenal insufficiency.

whyChain.why: Why must thionamide precede iodine?
whyChain.answer: Iodine is substrate for thyroid hormone synthesis. In Graves' disease, giving iodine alone could paradoxically INCREASE hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow effect). Thionamide first blocks organification, so the iodine can only block release without fueling new synthesis.

whyChain.why: Can iodine be given simultaneously with thionamide?
whyChain.answer: Recent evidence suggests simultaneous administration may be acceptable and might even improve outcomes. However, traditional teaching recommends 1+ hour delay to ensure thionamide effect. In practice, give thionamide immediately and iodine within 1-2 hours.

questions.stem: You are selecting a beta-blocker for this patient with thyroid storm and AFib with RVR.
questions.question: Which beta-blocker is preferred in thyroid storm and what is the dosing?
questions.hint: Consider which agent has additional T4→T3 conversion blockade.

options.text: Beta-blockers are contraindicated in thyroid storm
Propranolol (40-80mg PO q4-6h or IV) preferred because it also blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion; Esmolol IV for ICU (easily titratable)
Metoprolol is preferred
Atenolol once daily

questions.explanation: Propranolol is traditionally preferred: (1) Non-selective β-blockade for comprehensive symptom control; (2) At high doses (>160mg/day), blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion; (3) Dose: 40-80mg PO q4-6h, or 0.5-1mg IV slowly. Esmolol (IV, titratable, short-acting) is excellent in ICU. Both achieve rate control. This patient's HR 156 needs aggressive beta-blockade.

whyChain.why: What about her heart failure (bibasilar crackles)?
whyChain.answer: Beta-blockers in thyrotoxic heart failure can be tricky. However, tachycardia itself worsens heart failure by reducing diastolic filling time. Cautious beta-blockade (lower doses, esmolol for titratability) often IMPROVES cardiac output by allowing better ventricular filling. Monitor closely.

whyChain.why: What if beta-blockers are contraindicated?
whyChain.answer: True contraindications are rare in thyroid storm. For severe asthma, can use cardioselective agents (esmolol, metoprolol) carefully. Diltiazem is an alternative for rate control but doesn't block β-adrenergic symptoms. Reserpine (depletes catecholamines) is a historical alternative.

questions.stem: You are selecting between PTU and methimazole for this patient.
questions.question: Which thionamide is preferred for initial thyroid storm treatment and why?

options.text: Methimazole is always preferred due to fewer side effects
Both are equivalent in thyroid storm
Neither is used acutely
PTU preferred initially because it blocks both synthesis AND peripheral T4→T3 conversion; loading dose 500-1000mg, then 250mg q4h

questions.explanation: PTU advantages in thyroid storm: (1) Blocks thyroid hormone SYNTHESIS (like methimazole); (2) ALSO blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion (unlike methimazole) - T3 is the more active hormone; (3) PTU reduces T3 by 45% in first 24 hours vs only 10-15% for methimazole. Dosing: Loading 500-1000mg, then 250mg q4-6h. After stabilization, can switch to methimazole (better side effect profile).

whyChain.why: Why switch to methimazole later?
whyChain.answer: PTU has higher hepatotoxicity risk (boxed warning), higher frequency of vasculitis, and requires more frequent dosing (TID-QID vs daily). Methimazole is preferred for long-term management. The T4→T3 conversion blockade is most critical in acute storm.

whyChain.why: What if patient can't take oral medications?
whyChain.answer: For patients with altered mental status or GI dysfunction: PTU can be given rectally (same dose as oral), via NG tube, or compounding pharmacies can prepare IV formulations. Methimazole can similarly be given rectally or via NG.

questions.stem: You've given propranolol and PTU. Now you're planning iodine therapy.
questions.question: What is the mechanism and dosing of iodine in thyroid storm?

options.text: Radioactive iodine is given acutely
Iodine increases thyroid hormone production
Iodine only works in non-Graves' thyrotoxicosis
Wolff-Chaikoff effect: high iodine acutely inhibits thyroid hormone synthesis and release; give SSKI 5 drops (250mg) PO q6h or Lugol's solution 8 drops q6-8h

questions.explanation: Wolff-Chaikoff effect: High iodine concentrations paradoxically INHIBIT thyroid hormone synthesis and release. Works rapidly (within hours). Dosing: SSKI (saturated potassium iodide) 5 drops PO q6h, or Lugol's solution 8 drops q6-8h. Alternative: Sodium iodide 500-1000mg IV q12h. Effect is temporary (escape occurs in days-weeks), so definitive therapy still needed.

whyChain.why: What if patient has iodine allergy?
whyChain.answer: True iodine allergy is rare (usually contrast reactions involve hyperosmolarity or specific contrast molecules, not iodine itself). If genuine concern, lithium carbonate 300mg q8h can be used as alternative - it also inhibits thyroid hormone release.

whyChain.why: Why does escape occur?
whyChain.answer: The thyroid adapts to high iodine by downregulating the sodium-iodide symporter, reducing iodine uptake. This 'escape' from Wolff-Chaikoff occurs in 1-2 weeks. In thyroid storm, the acute effect is what matters; definitive therapy (surgery, RAI, or long-term ATD) prevents relapse.

questions.stem: You're adding glucocorticoids to the regimen.
questions.question: What is the rationale for glucocorticoids in thyroid storm?
questions.hint: Consider effects on hormone conversion and adrenal function.

options.text: Anti-inflammatory effect only
Glucocorticoids are contraindicated
Glucocorticoids increase thyroid hormone production
Blocks peripheral T4→T3 conversion AND treats relative adrenal insufficiency from hypermetabolic state; Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h or Dexamethasone 2mg IV q6h

questions.explanation: Glucocorticoids have dual benefit: (1) Block peripheral T4→T3 conversion (similar to PTU and propranolol); (2) Treat relative adrenal insufficiency - the hypermetabolic state of thyroid storm increases cortisol demand beyond adrenal capacity. Dosing: Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h (or Dexamethasone 2-4mg IV q6h if don't want mineralocorticoid). Give to ALL thyroid storm patients.

whyChain.why: Is adrenal insufficiency always present?
whyChain.answer: Not technically 'insufficiency' but relative inadequacy - normal adrenal output can't meet massively increased metabolic demands. Also, rapid thyroid hormone clearance in hyperthyroidism may accelerate cortisol metabolism. Empiric steroids are given regardless of cortisol levels.

whyChain.why: Why prefer dexamethasone sometimes?
whyChain.answer: Dexamethasone has no mineralocorticoid effect (if concerned about fluid retention in heart failure) and may have stronger T4→T3 conversion blockade. Hydrocortisone has more physiologic effects if true adrenal insufficiency is suspected.

questions.stem: The patient has fever of 39.8°C. You're managing hyperthermia.
questions.question: How should hyperthermia be managed in thyroid storm?
questions.hint: Consider both cooling and what to avoid.

options.text: Acetaminophen for fever; external cooling; AVOID aspirin/NSAIDs (displace thyroid hormone from binding proteins)
Ice water immersion is first-line
No treatment needed for fever
Aspirin is preferred for fever

questions.explanation: Hyperthermia management: (1) Acetaminophen is safe for fever; (2) External cooling (cooling blankets, ice packs) as needed; (3) AVOID aspirin and NSAIDs - they displace thyroid hormones from binding proteins, increasing FREE (active) hormone levels. This could worsen thyroid storm. Also consider treating precipitating infection (broad-spectrum antibiotics often given empirically).

whyChain.why: Why does aspirin worsen thyroid storm?
whyChain.answer: Thyroid hormones circulate mostly protein-bound (99.97% for T4). Aspirin competes for binding sites on TBG, albumin, and transthyretin. Displacement increases FREE T4 and T3, which are the biologically active forms. This can acutely worsen thyrotoxicosis.

whyChain.why: What about cholestyramine?
whyChain.answer: Cholestyramine (bile acid sequestrant) can be added to enhance thyroid hormone elimination. It binds T4/T3 in the gut, interrupting enterohepatic circulation. Dose: 4g PO QID. Used as adjunctive therapy when standard treatment inadequate.

sources.text: StatPearls: Thyroid Storm. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Despite 24 hours of aggressive treatment, the patient remains unstable.
questions.question: What options exist for refractory thyroid storm?
questions.hint: Consider procedures to rapidly remove thyroid hormone.

options.text: Plasmapheresis/plasma exchange to remove circulating hormone; Emergency thyroidectomy in select cases
Radioactive iodine ablation acutely
No additional options exist
Continue same treatment indefinitely

questions.explanation: Refractory thyroid storm options: (1) Plasmapheresis/therapeutic plasma exchange (TPE): Physically removes circulating thyroid hormones and autoantibodies. Can rapidly lower hormone levels as bridge to surgery or continued medical therapy; (2) Emergency thyroidectomy: Definitive removal of hormone source. Reserved for refractory cases, agranulocytosis (can't use thionamides), or severe drug reactions. Both require specialized resources.

whyChain.why: When is plasmapheresis indicated?
whyChain.answer: Consider when: (1) No response to 24-48 hours of maximal medical therapy; (2) Contraindication to thionamides (agranulocytosis, severe hepatotoxicity); (3) As bridge to surgery. TPE removes thyroid hormones, autoantibodies, and cytokines. Usually need multiple sessions.

whyChain.why: Is emergency surgery safe in thyroid storm?
whyChain.answer: Historically, surgery during active storm carried high mortality. With modern anesthesia and aggressive medical optimization (including plasmapheresis), emergency thyroidectomy is safer and may be life-saving in refractory cases. Goal is to optimize medically first if possible.

sources.text: StatPearls: Thyroid Storm. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Integrating all information about this case.
questions.question: What is the complete diagnosis and treatment plan for this patient?
questions.hint: Connect precipitants, diagnosis, and multimodal treatment.

options.text: Start radioactive iodine immediately

Thyroid storm (BWPS ~90) precipitated by infection + medication discontinuation; ICU admission; [1] Propranolol IV/PO, [2] PTU 500mg load then 250mg q4h, [3] SSKI 5 drops q6h (1 hour after PTU), [4] Hydrocortisone 100mg IV q8h; treat infection; monitor for improvement in 24-48 hours
Sepsis only, no thyroid treatment needed
Uncomplicated thyrotoxicosis, restart methimazole outpatient

questions.explanation: Complete synthesis: (1) Diagnosis: Thyroid storm (BWPS ~90: fever, AFib, CNS dysfunction, precipitants) in known Graves' disease; (2) Precipitants: Medication discontinuation + infection; (3) Treatment: ICU, multimodal therapy in correct sequence; (4) Supportive: acetaminophen for fever (avoid aspirin), treat infection, monitor for heart failure; (5) Response expected in 24-48 hours; (6) Plan definitive therapy (surgery or RAI) after stabilization.

whyChain.why: What is the expected timeline for improvement?
whyChain.answer: Beta-blocker effects: within hours. Thionamide effects: 1-2 days (blocks new synthesis but doesn't affect already-made hormone). Iodine effects: hours to days (blocks release). Most patients show clinical improvement within 24-48 hours. If no improvement, consider refractory management.

whyChain.why: What about long-term management?
whyChain.answer: After stabilization: (1) Switch PTU to methimazole for better safety profile; (2) Plan definitive therapy - usually thyroidectomy or radioactive iodine (RAI should wait until stable and ATD effect wanes); (3) Patient education about medication compliance; (4) Address underlying Graves' disease.

sources.text: StatPearls: Thyroid Storm. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Ross DS, et al. 2016 American Thyroid Association Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism. Thyroid. 2016;26(10):1343-1421. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected thyroid storm?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in thyroid storm. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Obtain a thorough history and physical examination.
correct.deeperWhy: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in thyroid storm. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in thyroid storm. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

B.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
B.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

C.misconception: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.
C.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
C.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

D.guidance: Consider why Obtain a thorough history and physical examination is the key concept.
D.bridgeTo: The correct answer is Obtain a thorough history and physical examination because it best fits the clinical scenario.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of thyroid storm. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Consider the differential diagnosis systematically.
correct.deeperWhy: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.
correct.clinicalPearl: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

mcqs.stem: When managing thyroid storm, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values.
correct.deeperWhy: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.
correct.clinicalPearl: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

mcqs.stem: A patient in DKA has a serum potassium of 3.1 mEq/L on presentation. What should be done BEFORE starting insulin?

options.text: Start bicarbonate infusion to correct the acidosis, which will normalize potassium
options.explanation: Bicarbonate shifts potassium intracellularly and would WORSEN hypokalemia. Routine bicarbonate is not recommended in DKA unless pH <6.9.

options.text: Give IV calcium gluconate for cardiac membrane stabilization, then start insulin
options.explanation: IV calcium stabilizes the myocardium in HYPERkalemia, not hypokalemia. For hypokalemia, direct potassium replacement is needed.

options.text: Replace potassium to ≥3.3 mEq/L first, as insulin will further lower potassium and risk cardiac arrest
options.explanation: Per ADA guidelines, if K+ <3.3 mEq/L, potassium must be replaced BEFORE insulin administration. Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and severe hypokalemia (K+ <3.0) can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Replace with IV KCl 20-40 mEq/hr until K+ ≥3.3, then start insulin.

options.text: Start insulin immediately — DKA is life-threatening and cannot be delayed
options.explanation: While DKA requires urgent treatment, giving insulin with K+ <3.3 mEq/L can precipitate fatal hypokalemia. The 15-30 minute delay to partially correct potassium is critical for patient safety.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Replace potassium to ≥3.3 mEq/L first, as insulin will further lower potassium and risk cardiac arrest.
correct.deeperWhy: Per ADA guidelines, if K+ <3.3 mEq/L, potassium must be replaced BEFORE insulin administration. Insulin drives potassium intracellularly, and severe hypokalemia (K+ <3.0) can cause fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Replace with IV KCl 20-40 mEq/hr until K+ ≥3.3, then start insulin.
